steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The claim is that caterpillar sounds are "directed primarily at predators." To support this, we need evidence that predators actually respond to — and are deterred by — those sounds, not just facts about the sounds themselves.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Caterpillar clicks were emitted in a frequency detectable by birds that prey on caterpillars, but caterpillar whistles were not.: describes acoustic properties of the sounds but says nothing about how predators react. ✗
  • Choice B — Each caterpillar species tended to produce one sound during simulated attacks, although individuals occasionally made a variety of other sounds during simulated attacks as well.: shows the sounds are produced reliably, but consistency alone doesn't prove they target predators. ✗
  • Choice C — Chickens and yellow warblers, two predators of caterpillars, have been observed to stop their attacks in response to caterpillar sounds.: chickens and yellow warblers cease attacks when they hear the sounds, directly showing a defensive effect on predators. ✓
  • Choice D — In most cases, the sound that a caterpillar species produced during simulated attacks was not produced by other caterpillar species during simulated attacks.: addresses which species produce the sounds, not how predators respond to them. ✗
That is the correct answer.
